summary.brief: Santana v. State. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: C was admitted to a state hospital for an induction of labor. During labor, it was determined that E was malpositioned and that a vaginal delivery would pose risks.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: C agreed to the manual rotation, which was unsuccessful. At that point, the delivery plan was changed to a cesarean section.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The cesarean section ultimately took an extended period of time, and E suffered permanent injuries in the process.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The expert opined in that letter, to a reasonable degree of medical certainty, that the state departed from the applicable standard of care in that it, among other things, failed to inform C regarding the risks of attempting a vaginal delivery.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert witness

evidence_index.text: The trial court denied the motion, concluding that the second count reasonably could be understood to sound in medical malpractice, and the state appealed.
evidence_index.speaker: Trial court conclusion

evidence_index.text: The term ‘‘medical malpractice claims’’ in § 4-160 (f) is broad enough to encompass a birthing mother’s allegation that she suffered emotional distress from physical injuries to her child.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: This court concluded that, when a fetus or infant suffers physical injuries as a result of medical malpractice during the labor and delivery process, the birthing mother is a joint victim of the malpractice.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: C was not precluded from asserting a medical malpractice claim against the state for damages based on purely emotional distress.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: To ascertain whether a claim qualifies as a medical malpractice claim, multiple factors are considered, including whether the defendant is being sued in his or her capacity as a medical professional.
evidence_index.speaker: Court reasoning

evidence_index.text: The trial court properly denied the state’s motion to dismiss that count of the complaint.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: C was admitted to a state hospital for an induction of labor. During labor, it was determined that E was malpositioned and that a vaginal delivery would pose risks. C agreed to a manual rotation, which was unsuccessful, leading to a cesarean section.

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Labor induction with complications due to fetal malposition

requestables.name: Fetal Sonography
requestables.description: Sonography to assess fetal position and growth

reveal.result_summary: Fetal weight above 90th percentile, abdominal circumference above 95th percentile
reveal.clinical_significance: Indicates fetal overgrowth, potential for obstructed labor
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt consideration of delivery options

requestables.name: Obstetrician Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with obstetrician regarding delivery options

reveal.result_summary: Counseling on delivery options including manual rotation and C-section
reveal.decision_impact: Should have led to earlier decision for C-section

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with elevated blood pressure and fetal overgrowth

timeline_phases.name: Labor Induction and Monitoring
timeline_phases.description: Patient undergoes labor induction and monitoring

timeline_phases.name: Decision on Delivery Method
timeline_phases.description: Decision-making regarding delivery method due to labor complications

key_events.event: Manual rotation attempted, then C-section decided

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in labor with fetal malposition and maternal exhaustion
decision_points.prompt: What delivery method should be chosen given the fetal malposition and maternal exhaustion?

options.clinical_reasoning: Given fetal malposition and maternal exhaustion, C-section is safer

options.description: Attempt manual rotation and continue with vaginal delivery
options.clinical_reasoning: Attempting manual rotation despite malposition and exhaustion

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant attempted manual rotation, then proceeded to C-section
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant believed manual rotation was feasible

expected_action_court.description: Immediate C-section should have been performed
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence indicated C-section was appropriate given the circumstances

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Manual rotation was inappropriate given the fetal position and maternal condition

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered permanent injuries due to extended cesarean section

key_findings: Breach of duty in delaying cesarean section
Causation established - delay led to extended procedure and injuries

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Prompt cesarean section would have prevented extended procedure time

factual_timeline.event: Patient diagnosed with elevated blood pressure, influenza, and proteinuria
Patient admitted for induction of labor
Manual rotation attempted, then C-section performed
Patient admitted for induction of labor

court_findings.finding: Breach of duty in delaying cesarean section
